name: Lean 4 Reference Counting — Atomic-vs-Persistent Sentinels
summary: Ullrich & de Moura's Lean 4 compiler (2019+) inserts explicit `inc`/`dec`/`reset`/`reuse` ops in IR (Perceus-like, predates Perceus formalisation). Distinctive low-level trick: `m_rc == 0` as a persistent sentinel + sign-bit for thread-shared. `isExclusive`-guarded primitives test RC == 1 and mutate in place. Cycles banned by language.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Ullrich & de Moura's Lean 4 compiler (2019+) inserts explicit `inc`/`dec`/`reset`/`reuse` ops in IR — Perceus-like in spirit, but predates the Perceus formalisation. Reuse analysis enables in-place mutation of unique arrays/strings.

Cycles are banned by language: pure Lean values cannot form mutable cycles, so no cycle collector is required. Comparable performance to OCaml in functional code; matches imperative when reuse fires. The combination — compile-time RC + reuse + cycle-free-by-construction — makes Lean 4 a particularly clean point in the design space, with the engineering trick described in the next section keeping the runtime cost low.

Status (as of 2026-04): production for several years; underpins the entire Lean 4 elaborator and tactic engine.

sections.title: Atomic-vs-persistent RC sentinels
sections.content:
The distinctive low-level trick is encoding three RC modes in one machine word: `m_rc == 0` as a persistent sentinel + sign-bit for thread-shared. Concretely:

- **Positive RC** — single-threaded fast path. `inc`/`dec` use plain non-atomic loads/stores. The vast majority of objects in idiomatic Lean code stay here.
- **Negative RC** — thread-shared. `inc`/`dec` use atomic operations. Once an object escapes to another thread, its sign flips permanently and it pays the atomic cost from then on.
- **Zero RC** — persistent / compact-region object. No RC traffic at all; the object lives in a region whose lifetime is managed independently (interned constants, compacted modules).

All defined in `lean.h`. The branch on the sign bit is one machine instruction and predicts well — the common case (positive RC) compiles to the same non-atomic increment a hand-written C program would emit. Compared to Swift (always atomic on bridged objects, sometimes non-atomic on Swift-native), Lean 4's discipline is more aggressive: a Lean object stays non-atomic until it provably escapes to another thread, at which point it pays the cost forever.

sections.title: isExclusive-guarded mutation and FFI considerations
sections.content:
`isExclusive`-guarded primitives — `Array.set`, `Array.swap`, persistent-array updates — test RC == 1 and mutate in place, else copy, giving pure functional semantics with imperative speed. `dbgTraceIfShared` helps locate accidental sharing during performance debugging. Persistent arrays (HAMT-style trie with a tail buffer mutated in place under unique RC, covered separately in `data/lean4-persistent-arrays`) are used pervasively in Lean's elaborator/tactic state — making RC a first-class enabler of an entire compiler architecture.

FFI considerations: Lean exposes `lean_object*` to C with explicit `lean_inc` / `lean_dec` / `lean_is_exclusive` functions. C code calling into Lean must respect the sign-bit convention — calling `lean_inc` on a thread-shared object must use the atomic path, on a thread-local one the non-atomic path. The convention matches the runtime helpers, but the FFI surface is the place where mistakes show up: a C extension that increments without checking the sign bit can either crash with a torn count under contention or silently undercount and cause use-after-free on the next decrement. The Lean documentation makes this explicit; production use is robust.
